nativeRange.habitat: The Regal Angelfish inhabits coral-rich areas of lagoons and seaward reefs, often sheltering near caves and ledges. It is found across a depth range of 0 to 110 m, though it is most commonly encountered above 48 m.

diet.notes: Specialised wild feeder on sponges and tunicates, which makes it notoriously difficult to convert to prepared foods. Aquarium diet should include sponge-based angelfish preparations, spirulina, marine algae, mysis shrimp, and finely chopped seafood, offered in frequent small meals.

avoidWith: highly aggressive fish that outcompete it for food

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 3614x2409. Tack-sharp in-situ Red Sea individual, whole fish in frame, vivid orange/blue-white bars, yellow tail, and blue-spotted dark dorsal patch all clearly shown.
H. Zell; 4277x2778. Sharp, dramatic dark-background shot with intensely saturated banding and full fin detail; whole fish visible.
Diego Delso; 4254x2836. Sharp in-situ Philippines individual, full lateral profile with clear diagnostic bars and yellow caudal fin; slightly cooler color cast than the recommended pick.

summary: The Regal Angelfish is a strikingly banded Indo-Pacific species prized for its vivid orange, blue, and yellow coloration, but it is widely considered one of the more challenging marine angelfish to keep, requiring an expert-level commitment to diet and water quality.
careNotes: The Regal Angelfish is one of the most challenging marine angelfishes to keep, and is generally considered expert-only. In the wild it feeds heavily on sponges and tunicates, and many specimens never fully transition to prepared foods in captivity. Survivability is reported to vary by collection region — specimens from the Red Sea and Maldives tend to acclimate more readily than those from the broader Indo-Pacific (Philippines, Indonesia), which historically have very high mortality rates; selecting locality and choosing actively feeding fish at point of sale strongly improves outcomes. Generally advised to introduce the Regal Angelfish first into the aquarium so it can establish a territory before more assertive fish are added. Provide a mature system with extensive live rock for grazing, caves and ledges for retreat, and open swimming space; an active swimmer that uses the full water column. Reef compatibility is mixed — it is commonly considered "reef-safe with caution": it has been known to nip at large-polyped stony corals, some soft corals, and clam mantles, but is generally tolerant of small-polyped stony corals and noxious soft corals. Like other large angelfishes, the Regal is reported to be susceptible to head and lateral line erosion (HLLE) when kept in poor water quality or fed a diet lacking marine algae and vitamins.
breedingNotes: Like other members of the angelfish family (Pomacanthidae), the Regal Angelfish has been reported to be a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals develop as females first and may later transition to male. Pair-bonded harems form on the reef, but captive breeding is essentially undocumented in home aquaria.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Designated secondary (Reef2Reef wiki) returned 403; swapped to FishBase per playbook. Cross-checked taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes), maximum length (25 cm SL), native range (Red Sea, East Africa to Tuamoto Islands; north to Ryukyu/Ogasawara; south to GBR/New Caledonia), wild temperature range (24.6–29 °C), depth (0–110 m), maximum reported lifespan (15 years), and diet (sponges and tunicates).

additional.name: Aquarium Specialty
additional.notes: Additional husbandry cross-check after Reef2Reef wiki 403 and Reefs.com 403. Confirmed adult size ~25 cm (10 in), water parameters (72–78 °F / 22.2–25.6 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), captive lifespan 10–15 years. Reported minimum tank size 100 US gal (~380 L) — lower than LiveAquaria's 125 US gal (~473 L); larger end biased to 500 L per tank-size rule given the species' active swimming.

fish.breedingNotes: The Regal Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite in the wild, meaning individuals typically develop as females before potentially transitioning to male. Captive breeding is essentially undocumented in home aquaria.
